q: Heat is the form of energy that can be transferred from one system to another as a result of temperature difference.
Heat transfer and thermodynamics are two independent and completely unrelated branches of science.
Thermodynamics deals with equilibrium states by outlining the relationship between energy and work.
One of the basic requirements for heat transfer is the presence of a temperature difference.
There can be a net heat transfer between two systems that are at the same temperature.
There are three basic ways of heat transfer; namely, heat conduction, heat convection and heat radiation.
In fluids, heat is mainly transferred by conduction process.
In convection, the motion of the fluid itself carries heat from one place to another.
In solids, heat is transferred by conduction process in which heat energy is transferred among the adjacent parts of the material without the motion of the material itself.
The third way to transfer heat energy is by radiation process in the form of mechanical waves.
Heat conduction is one mode of energy transfer.
The individual parts of a medium move as a whole in heat conduction.
Heat energy is transferred by successive distribution among the adjacent parts in heat conduction.
When heat is transferred via conduction, heat energy is transferred internally by vibrations of atoms and molecules.
Metals are generally poor conductors of heat because they lack free electrons.
The spoon which acts as a medium for heat transfer does not move at all during conduction.
The rate of heat conduction through a material of cross-sectional area $A$ is inversely proportional to the temperature difference $\Delta T$.
The rate of heat conduction is inversely proportional to the thickness $l$ of the material.
The constant $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the material, which is a measure of the ability of a material to conduct heat.
In SI units, $k$ is watt per metre per kelvin ($\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$).
The ratio $\frac{T_2 - T_1}{l}$ is called the thermal coefficient.
Temperature gradient can also be expressed in differential form as $\frac{\text{d}T}{\text{d}x}$.
In one dimensional form, the mathematical equation is written as $\frac{H}{A} = -k \frac{\text{d}T}{\text{d}x}$.
In the heat conduction formula, $\frac{H}{A}$ represents the heat current and $H$ represents the heat flow per unit area.
The mathematical model of heat conduction was first formulated by the French physicist Jean Baptiste Joseph Fourier.
Fourier's law states that the rate of heat flow per unit area is inversely proportional to the magnitude of temperature gradient.
A negative sign in Fourier's law indicates that temperature $T$ must decrease as distance $x$ increases.
In the SI units, heat flux density is watt per metre squared ($\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}$).
In the SI units, heat current is measured in joules per second squared.
A high value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal conductor.
According to Table 5.1, copper has a higher thermal conductivity than silver.
Materials such as copper and silver that are good electric conductors are also good heat conductors.
Materials such as rubber, wood, and styrofoam are good conductors of heat and have high conductivity values.
The major heat transfer in human beings is due to the flow of warm blood.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a fluid from places of higher temperature to places of lower temperature by movement of the fluid itself.
In heat convection, the transfer of energy occurs between an object and its environment due to solid contact.
Convection currents are set up in the fluid because the hotter part of the fluid is not as dense as the cooler part.
An upward buoyant force acts on the hotter fluid, making it rise while the cooler and denser fluid sinks.
Although some heat may be transferred by conduction in liquids and gases, a much larger quantity of heat is carried by the motion of the fluid itself.
Convection occurs in solids, liquids, and gases under equal conditions.
Convection occurs only in a liquid or a gas, never in a solid.
Buoyant force is what drives natural convection.
The basic equation for the rate of convection heat transfer is known as Fourier's law of cooling.
In the convection equation, $q$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient.
In SI units, $q$ is expressed as watt per metre squared per kelvin ($\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$).
When $T_2 > T_1$, heat is lost by the object and $H_{\text{conv}}$ is negative.
The heat loss by convection is important for living beings.
The household hot water system is designed based on the process of heat radiation in vacuum.
In household hot water tanks, hot water from the boiler rises and flows into the tank where it rises to the top.
Forced convection is required when the convective heat transfer coefficient of air is insufficient to cool a system.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that requires a dense material medium.
Heat is transferred by thermal radiation which are electromagnetic waves that pass through a medium or even vacuum.
Unlike conduction and convection, the transfer of energy by radiation does not require an intervening medium.
Energy transfer by radiation is the slowest and it suffers attenuation in a vacuum.
Thermal radiation is the form of radiation emitted by bodies because of their temperature.
All bodies at a temperature above absolute zero emit thermal radiation.
Radiation is mostly considered to be a volume phenomenon for solids that are opaque to thermal radiation.
The object which can absorb all the electromagnetic radiations falling upon it is called a blackbody.
The blackbody is not only a perfect absorber but also a best emitter of radiation.
The total emissive power of a blackbody is directly proportional to the square of absolute temperature.
Thermodynamics is the study of systems involving energy in the form of heat and work.
A gas confined by a piston in a cylinder that expands upon heating is demonstrating how a thermodynamic system can do work.
When two systems are in thermal equilibrium, there is a continuous net heat transfer between them to maintain the state.
Systems at the same temperature will be in thermal equilibrium with each other.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics states that when two objects are separately in thermodynamic equilibrium with a third object, they all are in thermodynamic equilibrium with each other.
The first law of thermodynamics is an application of the conservation energy for heat and thermodynamic processes.
In the equation $\Delta U = Q - W$, the value of $Q$ is positive when heat is removed from the system.
$W$ is positive if work is done by the system, and negative if work is done on the system.
The internal energy $U$ of a system is defined as the sum of the energies of all the constituent particles.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on volume and pressure, making temperature irrelevant to its value.
The internal energy of an ideal monatomic gas is given as $U = \frac{3}{2} n R T$.
Internal energy of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the absolute temperature.
Heat transfer and work are both energy in transit and neither is stored as such in a system.
Internal energy is a form of energy completely identical to either heat or work because both can change the internal energy of a system.
A thermodynamic process may be defined as a change from one equilibrium state of a system to another.
During a thermodynamic process, a system starting from an initial state passes through various quasistatic states to a final state.
In thermodynamics, a quasi-static process is a thermodynamic process that happens rapidly enough to bypass internal physical thermodynamic equilibrium.
An isobaric process occurs at constant pressure.
An example of an isobaric process is a gas, being slowly heated or cooled, confined by a piston in a cylinder.
The heat $Q$, the work $W$, or the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ are strictly zero in an isobaric process.
In an isobaric process the pressure is constant and the work is equal to the area of the shaded rectangle in a pressure vs volume graph.
An isochoric process occurs at constant pressure and varying volume.
When the volume of a thermodynamic system is constant, no work is done by the system because $W = p \Delta V = 0$.
According to the first law, the change in internal energy must equal any heat flowing into or out of the system in an isochoric process.
For an ideal monatomic gas, the molar heat capacity at constant volume is mathematically expressed as $C_V = \frac{5}{2} R$.
An isothermal process occurs at a constant temperature.
For a process to be isothermal, any heat flow into or out of the system must occur slowly to maintain the thermodynamic equilibrium of the system.
For an ideal gas, internal energy $U$ is a function of both temperature and pressure, ensuring $\Delta U$ remains non-zero in an isothermal process.
Since $\Delta U = 0$, according to the first law, $W = Q$ for an isothermal process.
If the system does the work, the energy comes from heat flowing into the system from the reservoir.
In an adiabatic process, there is continuous heat transfer into or out of the system.
An adiabatic process can be done either by enclosing the system in a thermal insulator or by carrying out the process very quickly.
The first law of thermodynamics $\Delta U = Q - W$ is reduced to $\Delta U = -W$ in an adiabatic process.
For an ideal gas, the work done in an adiabatic process can be calculated as $W = \frac{3}{2} n R (T_i - T_f)$.
Heat engine is a system that converts mechanical energy directly into heat, which is then stored in a reservoir.
A heat engine operates continuously and only heat and work may pass across its boundaries.
A series of processes in which a system is brought back to its initial state is called a cycle.
The thermal efficiency of a heat engine is definitively the ratio of heat input to work output.
Applying the first law of thermodynamic to one complete cycle, remembering that there is no net change of internal energy, we get $Q_H - Q_C = W$.
The transformation of heat into work is usually accomplished in practice by internal combustion engines and steam engines.
A gasoline engine generates the input heat by the combustion of fuel outside the engine cylinders.
In the gasoline engines, the combustion of the gasoline and air takes place explosively by an electric spark.
The gasoline engine cycle involves the performance of six processes, of which four require motion of the piston and are called strokes.
During the compression stroke, the mixture of gasoline vapour and air is expanded until its pressure drops considerably.
Combustion of the hot mixture is caused to take place very rapidly by an electric spark while the volume remains unchanged.
In the power stroke, the hot combustion products expand and push the piston out, thus suffering a drop in pressure and temperature.
An exhaust valve allows some gas to escape until the pressure drops to that of the atmosphere.
The theoretical efficiency of a gasoline engine is solely dependent on its internal temperature and unaffected by the compression ratio.
The diesel engine induces only air into the combustion chamber on its intake stroke.
In a diesel engine, diesel fuel requires an electric spark to ignite because the heat and pressure inside the cylinder are insufficient.
The Carnot engine operates on an ideal reversible cycle between two heat reservoirs.
Real engines are less efficient than the Carnot engine because they do not operate through a reversible cycle.
In an irreversible process, the system can be returned to its initial condition along the same path on the $p-V$ diagram.
The most important condition for a process to be reversible in thermodynamics is that the process should be carried out infinitesimally slowly.
The Carnot cycle consists of four reversible processes: two isothermal processes and two adiabatic processes.
In the Carnot cycle, step 2 is defined as reversible isothermal expansion.
During the adiabatic compression step in a Carnot engine, the temperature of the gas rises to $T_{H}$.
A corollary to Carnot's theorem states that all reversible engines operating between the same heat reservoirs are equally efficient.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a fluid by movement of the fluid itself.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that requires a material medium.
The rate of heat flow $H$ is the amount of heat energy transferred per unit time.
A blackbody is an object which can absorb all the electromagnetic radiations falling upon it.
The thermal efficiency of a heat engine is defined as the ratio of work input to heat output.
The Carnot engine was described by Nicolas Leonard Sadi Carnot in 1824.
In a reversible process, every point along the path on the $p-V$ diagram is in an equilibrium state.
During the isothermal expansion of a Carnot cycle, the gas absorbs heat from the high temperature reservoir.
No engine operating between two heat reservoirs can be more efficient than a Carnot engine operating between the same reservoirs.
Thermal conductivity is a measure of the ability of a material to conduct heat.
In a diesel engine cycle, the fuel injector is located near the air intake.
The Carnot cycle is considered a hypothetical engine cycle.
Gasoline engines typically operate between lower temperatures than diesel engines.
Isothermal processes in the Carnot cycle occur at constant temperatures.
The surroundings do work on the gas during the isothermal compression step.
The driving force in a reversible process must be large enough to maintain a state of equilibrium.
Heat conduction involves the movement of the individual parts of a medium as a whole.
The working substance of a Carnot engine is a real gas.
Efficiency of a heat engine can reach 100% theoretically.
The Carnot engine provides an upper limit for the efficiency of all real engines.
Absolute temperature is used when calculating the maximum theoretical efficiency.
The Carnot cycle is a reversible cycle.
